title: Semantic and keyboard-accessible components

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific React: Component-Based Browser Applications draft for Semantic and keyboard-accessible components; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.2; W3C Recommendation 2023; provisions applicable to Semantic and keyboard-accessible components
sources.claim: React output should use native semantic elements, accessible names, keyboard behavior, and state attributes just like authored HTML.

sources.locator: React Learn and Reference; React 19.2; provisions applicable to Semantic and keyboard-accessible components
sources.claim: React output should use native semantic elements, accessible names, keyboard behavior, and state attributes just like authored HTML.

canonicalExplanation.summary: React output should use native semantic elements, accessible names, keyboard behavior, and state attributes just like authored HTML.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A reusable dialog composes a real button trigger, labeled dialog region, focus management, and escape behavior. Your goal is to audit and repair component names, roles, states, focus order and keyboard behavior. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Routing and application context; Remote data and asynchronous UI; html accessibility audit; JSX expressions and attributes. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: React output should use native semantic elements, accessible names, keyboard behavior, and state attributes just like authored HTML. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Component abstraction can hide invalid nesting, generated ids must remain stable, and portals change DOM placement. This draft therefore rejects: Because React handles events, div elements are preferable to native buttons.

includes: Render native elements, visible focus and logical order instead of div-based imitation controls.
Audit and repair component names, roles, states, focus order and keyboard behavior.
excludes: Component abstraction can hide invalid nesting, generated ids must remain stable, and portals change DOM placement.

outcomes: Audit and repair component names, roles, states, focus order and keyboard behavior.

examples.title: Semantic and keyboard-accessible components: concrete case
examples.situation: A reusable dialog composes a real button trigger, labeled dialog region, focus management, and escape behavior.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A reusable dialog composes a real button trigger, labeled dialog region, focus management, and escape behavior.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Use native semantic controls in React; event handling does not supply keyboard, role, name, or state automatically.
examples.result: The result follows because Use native semantic controls in React; event handling does not supply keyboard, role, name, or state automatically.

examples.title: Semantic and keyboard-accessible components: boundary case
examples.situation: Component abstraction can hide invalid nesting, generated ids must remain stable, and portals change DOM placement.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Component abstraction can hide invalid nesting, generated ids must remain stable, and portals change DOM placement.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Because React handles events, div elements are preferable to native buttons.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Use native semantic controls in React; event handling does not supply keyboard, role, name, or state automatically.

analogy.example: A state-driven component workshop can model Semantic and keyboard-accessible components, where inputs and state produce a UI description.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Semantic and keyboard-accessible components before using exact platform terms
limitations: A workshop does not reproduce reconciliation identity, hook ordering, concurrent rendering, effects, or browser accessibility APIs.
